title: How Entrepreneurship Can Give You Your Power Back: A Mother's Journey
guestBio: Kathleen Reed is the founder of West Coast Myofunctional Therapy, a mother of three, and passionate advocate for whole-family wellness. She specializes in orofacial myofunctional therapy to support healthy growth, sleep, and vitality in children and adults.
excerpt: Discover how entrepreneur and mother Kathleen Reed reclaimed her power through business ownership while applying The Manage Her principles to transform her family life. A conversation about real leadership, invisible labor, and finding purpose beyond titles.
metaDescription: Learn how entrepreneurship gave Kathleen Reed her power back as she balances motherhood, business, and purposeful leadership without a fancy title.
keyTakeaways: Leadership exists everywhere—in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines—not just in boardrooms with fancy titles.
Entrepreneurship can be a powerful tool for reclaiming agency and control over your life as a mother and woman.
Making invisible labor visible in both homes and businesses creates more equitable and sustainable systems.
Technology and AI can help streamline work processes and reclaim precious time for what truly matters most.
Orofacial myofunctional therapy addresses foundational health issues that impact sleep, growth, and vitality for entire families.
Balancing motherhood, step-parenting, and business ownership requires intentional application of leadership principles at home.
Faith, family, and purpose can serve as guiding forces in both personal and professional decision-making.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership looks different in kitchens, carpool lines, and bedtime routines—and it's just as important as any boardroom decision.

timestamps.label: How The Manage Her transformed Kathleen's approach

content:
What if the power you've been searching for isn't waiting for you in a corner office or behind a fancy title? What if it's already within your reach, hiding in plain sight in the very roles you're already filling every single day?

This is exactly what Kathleen Reed discovered when she launched West Coast Myofunctional Therapy and began applying the principles from The Manage Her to both her business and family life. As a mother of three, step-parent, and wellness advocate, Kathleen's journey illuminates a truth that so many of us need to hear: entrepreneurship isn't just about building a business—it's about reclaiming your agency and designing a life that works for you.

The Hidden Leadership You're Already Practicing

Here's something that might surprise you: you're already a leader. Whether you realize it or not, you're making strategic decisions, managing complex systems, and influencing outcomes every single day. The difference is that much of this leadership happens in spaces that society doesn't always recognize or value—in kitchens during dinner prep, in carpool lines coordinating schedules, and in bedtime routines that set the tone for tomorrow.

Kathleen's story beautifully illustrates this concept. After reading The Manage Her cover to cover, she began to see her daily management of family life through a completely different lens. The invisible labor she was performing—the mental load of coordinating schedules, anticipating needs, and ensuring everyone's wellbeing—wasn't just busy work. It was sophisticated project management and strategic leadership in action.

When you start to recognize the complexity and skill involved in managing a household, you begin to understand that you already possess many of the capabilities needed to lead in any context. The question isn't whether you have leadership potential—it's how you're going to channel it.

Entrepreneurship as Empowerment

For Kathleen, launching her myofunctional therapy practice wasn't just about career fulfillment—it was about taking back control. Entrepreneurship became her pathway to designing work that aligned with her values, her family's needs, and her vision for making a meaningful impact in the world.

Orofacial myofunctional therapy might sound technical, but at its core, it's about helping families thrive. By addressing foundational issues related to breathing, sleep, and oral function, Kathleen's work impacts entire family systems. Children sleep better, parents feel more rested, and everyone's quality of life improves. It's a perfect example of how entrepreneurship can be both personally empowering and deeply service-oriented.

The beauty of building your own business is that you get to define success on your own terms. You can create systems that work for your life rather than forcing your life to fit into someone else's system. This is particularly powerful for mothers who often feel torn between competing demands and expectations.

Making the Invisible Visible

One of the most transformative concepts from The Manage Her is the idea of making invisible work visible—and Kathleen has embraced this principle wholeheartedly. In her family life, this means having honest conversations about who does what and why. It means recognizing and valuing the mental and emotional labor that keeps households running smoothly.

In her business, making invisible work visible translates to clear processes, documented systems, and leveraging technology to handle routine tasks. By using AI and other tools to streamline administrative work, Kathleen frees up time and mental energy for the high-impact activities that truly matter—both in her practice and with her family.

This approach isn't about perfection or having it all figured out. It's about being intentional and strategic with your time and energy. When you can see all the work that's happening, you can make better decisions about what to keep, what to delegate, and what to eliminate entirely.

Technology as a Time Freedom Tool

Here's where things get really exciting: we're living in an era where technology can genuinely help us reclaim time for what matters most. Kathleen's integration of AI and other tools into her practice isn't about replacing the human element—it's about amplifying it.

When routine tasks are handled efficiently by technology, there's more space for the work that requires human insight, creativity, and connection. For Kathleen, this means more time for direct patient care, more presence with her family, and more energy for the off-grid adventures that bring her family joy and restoration.

The key is approaching technology strategically rather than letting it overwhelm or distract you. Ask yourself: how can these tools serve your bigger vision rather than becoming another thing to manage?

Finding Rest in Purpose

Perhaps one of the most refreshing aspects of Kathleen's approach is how she's integrated rest and restoration into her family's rhythm. Their off-grid van adventures aren't just vacations—they're intentional breaks from the noise and busyness that can consume our daily lives.

This commitment to rest reflects a deeper understanding of sustainable leadership. You can't pour from an empty cup, and you can't lead effectively if you're constantly running on fumes. By modeling healthy boundaries and prioritizing family restoration time, Kathleen is teaching her children valuable lessons about work-life integration and self-care.

Leadership Rooted in Faith and Family

What ties everything together in Kathleen's story is her clear sense of purpose rooted in faith and family values. This isn't about having all the answers—it's about having a North Star that guides decision-making in both business and personal contexts.

When your entrepreneurial journey is grounded in something bigger than profit or personal achievement, it becomes more resilient and more fulfilling. You're not just building a business; you're creating something that reflects your values and contributes to the wellbeing of others.

Your power has never been missing—it's been waiting for you to recognize it, claim it, and direct it toward what matters most to you. Whether that's through entrepreneurship, enhanced leadership in your current role, or simply a more intentional approach to managing your daily life, the choice is yours to make.
